ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers herbal infusions broadly; no specific chrysanthemum honey lemon tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 covers herbal tea as a substance class; no specific chrysanthemum tea with honey and lemon code exists.

preparation_method: served cold or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One large takeaway cup of approximately 500 ml, as served by KOI Thé in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chrysanthemum Tea, brewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented with honey and lemon juice nutrient data from USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for wildflower honey lemon chrysanthemum tea exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for brewed chrysanthemum tea (plain herbal infusion, ~1–2 kcal/100 ml), wildflower honey (~304 kcal/100 g), and lemon juice (~22 kcal/100 ml). The large KOI Thé serving (500 ml) is estimated to contain approximately 30–40 ml honey equivalent and 20 ml lemon juice diluted in brewed tea. Per-100 g values are derived from this blended composition. Confidence is medium due to estimation from components.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; chrysanthemum tea base treated as negligible GI herbal infusion
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for wildflower honey lemon chrysanthemum tea. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on: (1) plain chrysanthemum tea has negligible GI as a herbal infusion with no glycemic carbohydrates; (2) wildflower honey has a published GI of approximately 45–55 per University of Sydney GI Database (honey, various floral sources); (3) lemon juice contributes negligible GI. The blended beverage GI is dominated by the honey component and is estimated at 45 (low end of honey GI range). Glycemic load per 500 ml serving calculated as: (7.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 500 g serving × GI 45) / 100 = 15.75, rounded to 15.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~10 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: This beverage contains negligible sodium (~2 mg/100 ml) and poses minimal concern for individuals managing hypertension; however, the added honey contributes free sugars which should be considered in the context of overall dietary sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Free sugars from wildflower honey (~34 g per 500 ml serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this beverage with caution as the wildflower honey contributes approximately 34 g of free sugars per large serving, which can raise postprandial blood glucose; portion control or requesting reduced honey is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and sodium; antioxidant contribution from chrysanthemum and lemon
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage is essentially fat-free and very low in sodium, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; chrysanthemum flowers and lemon juice provide flavonoids and vitamin C with potential antioxidant benefit, though the honey-derived free sugar content should be moderated in individuals with metabolic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wildflower Honey Lemon Chrysanthemum Tea (Large) is a Taiwanese-style sweetened herbal tea beverage sold by KOI Thé, a popular Taiwanese bubble tea chain with outlets across Singapore. The drink is prepared by steeping dried chrysanthemum flowers (Chrysanthemum morifolium) in hot water to produce a pale golden, lightly floral infusion, which is then sweetened with wildflower honey and brightened with fresh lemon juice before being served chilled or at room temperature in a large 500 ml cup. The flavour profile is delicately floral and subtly sweet with a refreshing citrus note; the texture is that of a light, clear tea with no solids. Chrysanthemum tea is a traditional Chinese herbal beverage widely consumed across Taiwan, Hong Kong, and Southeast Asia, prized for its cooling properties in traditional Chinese medicine and its mild antioxidant content from flavonoids such as luteolin and apigenin. The addition of wildflower honey replaces refined sugar and contributes trace minerals and a complex floral sweetness, while lemon juice adds vitamin C and acidity. Nutritionally, the beverage is low in calories (~140 kcal per 500 ml serving), contains negligible fat and protein, and derives most of its energy from the free sugars in honey (~34 g per serving). The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), driven by the honey component. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate intake due to the free sugar load from honey; the beverage is otherwise low in sodium and saturated fat, making it broadly suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management when consumed in moderation.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for wildflower honey lemon chrysanthemum tea exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by component composition using USDA FoodData Central data for brewed herbal tea, wildflower honey, and lemon juice. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to published honey GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated: brewed chrysanthemum tea base (~1 kcal/100 ml, negligible macros) combined with wildflower honey (~30–35 ml per 500 ml serving, contributing ~90–105 kcal and ~25–30 g sugar) and lemon juice (~15–20 ml per serving). Per-100 g values reflect the blended beverage composition. 2. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero, as this is an aqueous herbal tea beverage with honey and lemon; no fat-containing ingredients are present. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed herbal tea, honey, and lemon juice. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are all 0.0: naturally zero by the fat-free nature of all component ingredients. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as the beverage is a strained liquid infusion with no particulate plant matter retained. 5. protein_g is 0.1: trace amount from honey and chrysanthemum infusion; effectively negligible. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are all -1: not reported or not reliably quantifiable for this estimated blended beverage composition in the approved source databases. 7. glycemic_index set to 45 (low) as an analogous estimate anchored to wildflower honey GI (~45–55) from the University of Sydney GI Database; chrysanthemum tea base contributes negligible GI. 8. Vegan flag is false because the beverage contains honey. 9. KOI Thé is a Taiwanese chain; this item is classified as Taiwanese cuisine served in Singapore. Multilingual names follow the Taiwanese cuisine rule; however, given the Singapore serving context, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are also provided to support Singaporean multilingual retrieval. 10. The 'L' prefix in the input food_name refers to the large cup size (大杯), reflected in the canonical food name and local names.
